text:
Abstract. This paper introduces a type of neural network model that can deal with the large scale relational learning problem by embedding multi-relational graphs into a continuous vector space. As the knowledge in many fields like computational biology and natural language processing increases rapidly and becomes highly structured, symbolic representations can become increasingly complicated and noisy, and thus very hard to deal with. In this paper, we name the energy function we used for this purpose as "semantic matching energy" (SME), which could embed the representations of entities and relation types into one low dimensional vector space. While other models represent each relation by a matrix, our model represent the relation type by a vector, which saves a lot of parameters, and it is proved that our model could easily handle relation types that exceeds thousands.

We train the model to optimize a stochastic ranking objective which gives higher probability to the seen triples from the multi-relational graph. Our model outperforms several baselines on the link prediction and entity ranking tasks on benchmarks such as WordNet and Kinships datasets. We also apply our method to an open-text semantic parsing task. With our joint words and senses embedding learning approach from diverse set of sources including Wikipedia and WordNet, the model performs on large-scale all-words word-sense disambiguation task that has thousands of nodes and relations, while it improves over the state-of-the-art unsupervised word sense induction baseline. Experimental verification of the method suggests that semantic matching energy functions are strong inductives of relational semantics even for the highly-competitiveness and noisy ambient spaces of the modern era and large dimensions.
